AI Generated Image — A difficult path between immutability vs mutation with discipline
If you spend enough time in the frontend ecosystem, you’ll eventually encounter statements like:

• “Never mutate state.”

• “Mutation is evil.”

• “Always return new objects.”

• “Pure functions are safer.”
These ideas are often presented as universal truths. But are they really?
Or have we simply inherited concepts from other languages, paradigms, and architectures without questioning whether they fit the JavaScript ecosystem and the browser runtime?
JavaScript Was Never a Functional Language
JavaScript is a multiparadigm language. It supports functional programming, but it was not designed around immutability or purity.
The web itself is fundamentally mutable:
element.style.color = "red";array.push(item);
map.set(key, value);
The DOM is mutable.
Maps are mutable.
Sets are mutable.
Dates are mutable.
The browser APIs are mutable.
Mutation isn’t an accident in JavaScript. It’s the default model. Yet somewhere along the way, mutation became almost taboo. How did we get here?

Borrowing Ideas From Other Worlds
Many of the concepts we use today originated in environments with very different constraints.
Functional languages like Haskell embraced immutability because purity enables strong guarantees and makes concurrency easier.
Object-oriented enterprise languages introduced dependency injection containers and layers to manage extremely large systems.
C# and Java popularized extensive type systems because they lacked JavaScript’s dynamic nature.
None of these ideas are inherently bad. The problem appears when solutions become dogma.
Sometimes we inherit answers before questioning whether we actually have the same problems.
React Changed Everything
One of the biggest reasons immutability became so popular wasn’t JavaScript itself. It was React.
React’s rendering model heavily relies on reference equality:
prevState !== nextStateDetecting changes through immutable objects is extremely efficient.
So immutable updates became the recommended pattern:
setState({
...state,
items: [...state.items, item]
});Over time, people stopped seeing this as a React optimization and started treating it as a JavaScript best practice.
But React’s architecture is not JavaScript.
It is one particular framework with one particular set of trade-offs.
Vue, MobX, Solid and many reactive systems embrace mutation and work perfectly fine.
The rule wasn’t universal.
The architecture was.
Mutation Isn’t The Enemy
Mutation itself is not dangerous. Shared mutation is.
There is a huge difference between:
user.name = "John";inside a local scope and a mutable global object touched by dozens of components.
Most bugs attributed to mutation are actually problems of coupling and ownership.
Who owns this state?
Who is allowed to change it?
Who observes it?
These questions matter far more than whether a property assignment happened.
Immutability Has Costs
Immutability isn’t free. The cost usually isn’t performance. It’s complexity.
Imagine a simple state:
const state = {
user: {
profile: {
settings: {
theme: "light"
}
}
}
};Changing the theme with mutation is straightforward:
state.user.profile.settings.theme = "dark";The intent is obvious.
With immutable updates, preserving every parent reference often requires rebuilding the entire path:
state = {
...state,
user: {
...state.user,
profile: {
...state.user.profile,
settings: {
...state.user.profile.settings,
theme: "dark"
}
}
}
};The point isn’t that immutability is bad. It’s that immutability introduces its own costs.
And sometimes those costs exist not because the problem itself is complex, but because a particular architecture relies on reference comparisons to detect changes.
In those cases, we’re not necessarily solving a business problem.
We’re satisfying the needs of the framework.

Browser Applications Are Different
Frontend applications run inside a mutable environment.
The DOM is mutable.
User interactions are mutable.
Timers, events, network requests and browser APIs are stateful.
Trying to force absolute functional purity into this world often means fighting the platform rather than embracing it.
Perhaps the goal shouldn’t be eliminating mutation.
Perhaps it should be controlling it.
Local Mutation, Stable Boundaries
A more pragmatic approach might be:

• Mutate locally.

• Keep boundaries predictable.

• Prefer pure functions when they simplify things.

• Avoid shared mutable state.

• Use immutability where it provides value, not because it became dogma.
Because engineering is about trade-offs, not ideologies.
When the Cure Is Bigger Than the Disease
Over the years, the frontend ecosystem has also seen the rise of tools and even entirely new languages designed around these ideas. Projects like Elm embraced immutability and functional programming from the ground up, promising safer and more predictable applications.
While these approaches can provide real benefits, they also introduce costs that are easy to underestimate. Adopting a new language means asking teams to learn new mental models, abandon part of the existing JavaScript ecosystem, depend on smaller communities, and accept additional tooling and integration challenges.
In some cases, we end up adding layers of complexity to solve problems that the platform itself may not naturally have. Ironically, the pursuit of simplicity can sometimes lead us to environments that are significantly more complex than the one we started with.
The question isn’t whether these tools are good or bad, but whether the problems they solve justify the cognitive, organizational, and ecosystem costs they introduce.
Mutation Without Losing Predictability
Not every reactive system needs immutable updates.
In Jails, state changes are explicit, but they don’t require rebuilding entire object trees just to notify the UI.
For example, changing a local state for a Component:
import { type Component } from 'jails-js/types'export default function myComponent ({ main }: Component) {
main(() => {
on('click', 'button[data-add]', onadd)
})
const onadd = () => {
state.set(s => s.counter += 1)
}
}
export const model = {
counter: 0
}
Or using a Store for shared state with jails.stdlib/store :
import { Store } from 'jails.stdlib/store'const initialState = {
user: {
profile: {
theme: 'light'
}
}
}
export const mystore = Store( initialState, {
SET_THEME( state, payload ) {
// ...Validations on payload data
state.user.profile.theme = payload.theme
// The returned object will be merged with actual state internally
return state
}
})
import { mystore } from '../stores/my-store'//Listening to store changes
store.subscribe({ SET_THEME : (state, { action, payload }) => console.log(state, action, payload) })
//Firing the change
store.dispatch('SET_THEME', { theme: 'dark' })
The business operation itself is simple: changing a property.
Jails embraces that simplicity by treating mutation as an implementation detail while keeping state changes explicit and observable.
This illustrates an important point: immutability is one way to achieve predictability, but it isn’t the only one. Different architectures make different trade-offs, and those trade-offs shouldn’t be confused with universal principles.
Mutation Is Not the Problem, Ownership Is
A common criticism of mutable state is that changes can accidentally leak into other parts of the application through shared references. This concern is legitimate, but it is important to recognize that shallow copies are not a limitation of a particular library, they are a characteristic of JavaScript itself.
Even immutable update patterns rely heavily on shallow copying:
const nextState = {
...state
};Nested objects are still shared unless every level is rebuilt. In other words, immutability doesn’t eliminate references; it simply manages them differently.
The more fundamental question isn’t whether mutation exists, but who owns the data and who is allowed to modify it.
A system where changes happen through a small number of explicit entry points is often easier to reason about than one where objects are recreated everywhere, but ownership remains unclear. Predictability comes less from forbidding mutations and more from making them intentional, centralized, and observable.
In that sense, encapsulation matters more than immutability. The goal is not to remove state changes from the system, the browser itself is built around mutable APIs, but to ensure that changes happen where they are expected to happen, making them easier to understand and track.
In the case of Jails, state access is intentionally constrained to 3 possible interfaces:

/* Write */
state.set(( currentState ) => { ... })
SET_THEME( state, payload ) { }/* Read */
store.subscribe(( state, { action, payload })
This creates a clear separation of responsibilities. The subscriber acts as a read-only observation point. Its purpose is to react to state changes, not to produce them, so mutations are never expected there.
On the other hand, state.set() and store actions are explicitly designed as the place where state transitions occur. If something changes, this is where changes are expected to happen. This distinction makes ownership and intent obvious: subscriptions observe, while state setters modify.
As a result, when something goes wrong and an investigation is needed, there is no need to search the entire application for arbitrary object mutations. State setters naturally becomes one of the first places to look. By restricting where mutations are expected, debugging becomes a matter of tracing a few well-defined entry points rather than reasoning about an entire object graph.
In other words, predictability comes not from eliminating mutations, but from making them explicit and easy to locate.
And when those expectations fail, debugging tools continue to evolve. Modern AI assistants are becoming increasingly capable of helping developers trace state changes, inspect call stacks, and identify the origin of bugs.
Since debugging often consumes far more engineering time than writing code itself, perhaps the future lies not in trying to eliminate every possible mistake through increasingly restrictive patterns, but in making failures easier to diagnose and recover from.

Maybe We Imported More Than We Needed
Frontend development has inherited many ideas from other ecosystems:

• Immutability from functional languages.

• OOP, Dependency injection Containers from enterprise Java.

• Layered architectures from backend systems.

• Heavy typing from statically typed languages.
Sometimes these ideas solve real problems.
Sometimes they solve problems we don’t actually have.
The interesting question isn’t whether these concepts are good or bad. It’s whether we’re applying them because they fit our environment.
Or simply because we inherited them.
